input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Lab‑scale gantry crane operated in a controlled laboratory setting; the physical side consists of a metal gantry structure, a hoist carriage, two linear axes and a rotary joint driven by an embedded controller box.  The crane is a compact material handling rig with well‑defined translational and rotational kinematics, making it suitable for open‑source research and educational purposes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin delivers continuous monitoring of shaft positions and velocity, a closed‑loop optimal‑control module that computes actuator commands based on the kinematic model, a historical data‑logging service recording all sensor streams, a data‑visualization interface rendering CAD geometry and motion trajectories, and a continuous‑validation routine that compares live measurements against the kinematic model to detect deviations.  These services target researchers, educators, and operational staff for debugging, performance improvement, and model validation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation uses openly available software: a CAD package for geometry generation, a kinematic modeling engine for forward‑ and inverse‑kinematics, a lightweight time‑series database for logging, and web‑based dashboards for visualization.  The twin communicates via a standard cyber‑physical communication stack (e.g., MQTT or OPC‑UA), but no proprietary tools are required.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: GreenhouseDT orchestrates its digital twin through containerised services running on virtual machines, with sensor data ingested and persisted in an InfluxDB time‑series database; inter‑component communication is handled by an Apache ActiveMQ message broker using a publish‑subscribe model that supports synchronous state updates and real‑time control loops within the self‑adaptive twin.  The Air Quality Management exemplar employs a Raspberry‑Pi sensor suite whose telemetry is transmitted via MQTT to Microsoft Azure IoT services, after which an AutomationML‑based digital twin model is visualised and simulated through Azure‑hosted components.  Both exemplars adopt lightweight containerisation or virtualization strategies, allowing isolated deployment, straightforward scaling, and consistent coordination between physical and virtual subsystems.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: GreenhouseDT integrates with external systems through its Apache ActiveMQ broker, exposing JMS interfaces, and with InfluxDB’s HTTP and Line Protocol APIs for time‑series data ingestion and retrieval; these interfaces enable data exchange with third‑party logging, monitoring or analytics tools.  The Air Quality Management exemplar leverages Microsoft Azure’s cloud stack, where sensor telemetry is sent via secure MQTT to Azure IoT Hub, then processed by Azure stream analytics feeding the AutomationML model stored in Azure Blob Storage; downstream enterprise applications can retrieve the twin data via Azure’s REST APIs.  This combination of open‑source broker, time‑series database, and commercial cloud platform provides horizontal connectivity to data sources, analytics services and potential enterprise ERP or MES systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: GreenhouseDT utilizes an InfluxDB time‑series database for storing experimental greenhouse data and an ActiveMQ message broker for communication between the physical greenhouse sensors, actuators, and the digital‑twin simulation. The air‑quality exemplar defines its twin using AutomationML, orchestrated through Microsoft Azure tools and Azure‑based data pipelines. Both stacks combine containerised virtual‑machine images and pre‑recorded data feeds to enable behavioural adaptation of the analogue system and its digital counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Physical Crane Interface, implemented in Python, continuously samples state data (e.g., motor positions, velocities, and other sensor readings) from the crane controllers. This data is sent to the digital twin by writing to the TimescaleDB database, which is accessed by the Logging Application. The data is likely transmitted in a structured numeric format such as JSON or binary; the document does not detail specific data types or schemas. Transmission occurs in a continuous stream, typically bound to the update cycle of the crane's sensors. Event triggers include periodic polling and change‑detection, with logs timestamped for audit. No explicit data‑quality checks, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms beyond the database insertion are described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Digital twin services generate control plans: the Trajectory Generator Service computes optimal movement trajectories, and the Simulation Service validates them. The resulting trajectory commands are offered to the Control Application, which then uses the Physical Crane Interface to send commands to the physical crane. Command formats are unspecified but are likely serialized in a Python‑friendly structure (e.g., JSON or binary). The document does not report on validation, authentication, conflict‑resolution, or confirmation mechanisms after command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All intra‑system communication between digital twin services is mediated by an MQTT broker, providing publish‑subscribe messaging for event signaling. The Physical Crane Interface communicates directly with the crane controllers via an unspecified protocol (possibly serial or Ethernet). The network topology is essentially a star, with all services connecting to the MQTT broker and the database. Bandwidth, latency, and QoS requirements are not stated, and no security protocols (TLS, authentication, etc.) are detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The system appears to be deployed on on‑premises infrastructure (local server or edge device) running the Python applications, MQTT broker, TimescaleDB database, and Grafana dashboard. No cloud deployment, scalability mechanisms, or availability/reliability guarantees are explicitly described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The GreenhouseDT exemplar is developed and distributed via a GitHub repository, using Git for version control. Modeling is performed using AutomationML, providing a structured digital twin specification. The Raspberry‑Pi serves as the on‑board sensor subsystem and streams data over an ActiveMQ message broker to the twin. Sensor data is stored in an InfluxDB time‑series database, while the twin’s control logic runs in virtual machine images that contain prerecorded data for reproducibility. The deployment stack relies on containerization or virtualization to facilitate rapid installation, but explicit testing and quality assurance procedures are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The exemplar offers multiple application scenarios: runtime visualization of greenhouse or indoor air quality metrics, simulation of the greenhouse environment, and predictive modelling to anticipate virus spread during indoor air quality management. Azure Tools are used to implement the digital twin pipeline, enabling data ingestion, analytics and dashboard rendering. Detailed analytics or confidence measures for decision recommendations are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The digital twin model conforms to the AutomationML industry standard. The exemplar uses open‑source technologies such as InfluxDB and ActiveMQ; no adherence to commercial twin framework or additional certification requirements is described.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_
